title: Buttermilk scones
subtitle: Classic British plain scones with a high, even rise
excerpt: Plain British scones made with buttermilk for a tender crumb and reliable rise. The dough is handled as little as possible, cut with a round cutter, and baked at high heat until well-risen and golden. Serve split with clotted cream and jam.
sourceNotes: Plain scones are a British baking staple documented in Victorian household cookery. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, includes several scone recipes. The use of buttermilk with a raising agent is an older method than cream scones; the acid in buttermilk reacts with bicarbonate of soda or baking powder to give a lighter, more tender crumb than milk alone. The clotted cream and jam accompaniment is associated with Cornish and Devonshire cream teas.

content.text: The entire skill in scone-making is restraint. The

is the difference between a well-risen scone with a soft crumb and a tough, dense one. Mix just enough to combine, pat rather than roll, and cut without twisting. The buttermilk adds tenderness; the high heat provides lift.

Preheat the oven to 220°C conventional. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Sift the flour, baking powder, salt, and sugar into a large bowl. Add the cold butter and rub in quickly until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs with some pea-sized pieces remaining. Make a well in the centre and pour in the buttermilk. Stir briefly with a knife until the dough just comes together; do not overwork it. Turn out onto a lightly floured surface and pat gently to 2.5 cm thick. Cut rounds with a 6 cm cutter, pressing straight down without twisting. Place on the tray close together. Brush the tops with beaten egg, taking care not to let it drip down the sides. Bake for 10 to 12 minutes until risen and golden. Transfer to a rack.
